summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft should make Windows 8 on ARM a Metro-only platform, forbidding classic desktop apps even if recompiled. He contends that CPU architecture transitions inevitably confuse consumers, and the simplest distinction for users is 'Metro apps run everywhere, classic apps run only on Intel.' He draws an analogy to Apple's Flash strategy on iOS: developers only undertake the hard work of building native replacements when they must, not when they should. By making ARM tablets Metro-only, Microsoft would create a compelling forcing function for developer adoption of Metro, much as iOS forced the web away from Flash.
key_points: No matter what Microsoft decides, ARM-based Windows 8 machines won't run existing x86 apps, so consumer confusion is inevitable either way
The cleanest way to minimize confusion is a simple rule: Metro runs everywhere, classic desktop runs only on Intel
Developers follow the path of least resistance — they'll only rewrite apps for Metro if recompiling for the classic desktop on ARM isn't an option
Apple's iOS proves the principle: banning Flash forced developers to build native and web-standards alternatives in ways that merely discouraging Flash on the Mac never did
Microsoft faces the same Flash/plugin dilemma within Metro IE itself, reinforcing the pattern that 'must' drives adoption better than 'should'
money_quotes: Normal people do not understand what CPU architectures or binary instruction sets are.

So Microsoft's choice is between (a) asking users to tell the difference between classic and Metro apps, or (b) asking users to tell the difference between classic apps that have been recompiled for ARM and those which are Intel-only.
Developers are more likely to undertake more work if they must rather than merely should.
There are far more websites and online services that have undertaken the work to support iOS with non-Flash solutions than there are for the Mac.
MLB has excellent native apps for watching live baseball games on the iPhone and iPad. On the Mac, they require the user to install Flash.
Otherwise, too many developers will think, Why rewrite and redesign my entire app when I can just recompile it for the classic Windows desktop on ARM?
Windows 8 supports an incredibly wide range of hardware, and Metro runs everywhere. But our most advanced mobile hardware designs are Metro only.
key_insight: Platform transitions succeed when developers are forced to adopt the new paradigm rather than given an easy path to carry forward the old one.
